title: H-WM: Robotic Task and Motion Planning Guided by Hierarchical World Model
authors: Jinbang Huang; Wenyuan Chen; Zhiyuan Li; Oscar Pang; Xiao Hu; Lingfeng Zhang; Yuanzhao Hu; Zhanguang Zhang; Mark Coates; Tongtong Cao; Xingyue Quan; Yingxue Zhang
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2602.11291 in Feb 2026; supplied web version v2 dated 4 Mar 2026
open_source_notes: No public implementation repository was identified on the paper page.
task_domain: Long-Horizon Manipulation; Task and Motion Planning; Vision-Language-Action Control
primary_problem: Hierarchical World Modeling; Symbolic Planning; Visual Subgoal Prediction
world_model_detail: A fine-tuned LLM predicts structured logical actions and state transitions, while a latent visual world model predicts end-of-subtask visual goal features conditioned on the observation, joint state, logical action, and predicted logical state.
predictive_space: Hybrid: symbolic logic and latent visual state
prediction_target: Future logical actions and states; terminal visual subgoal embeddings
video_prediction: No — predicts one latent visual subgoal per symbolic subtask
planning_method: Hierarchical; Learned Symbolic Search; Language Planner
planning_representation: Logical state-action trajectories plus latent visual subgoals
horizon_basis: LIBERO-LoHo contains 5–7 logical steps, RoboCerebra reaches 20 steps, and the real-robot task contains eight steps.
replanning_online: Yes — logical actions and visual subgoals are generated at subtask boundaries and completion prediction advances the plan
learning_type: LLM Fine-Tuning; Latent Visual Prediction; VLA Guidance
pretraining: Yes — pretrained LLM, frozen visual encoder, and pretrained VLA policies
observation_modality: RGB; robot joint configuration; symbolic predicates; natural-language instruction
goal_representation: Logical goal state and predicted latent visual subgoals
action_space: Logical actions at the high level; continuous robot actions from the downstream VLA
benchmark: LIBERO-10; LIBERO-LoHo; RoboCerebra; real eight-step table setup/cleaning task
robot_platform: Simulated LIBERO and RoboCerebra manipulators; UR5e with RealSense camera
main_contribution: Jointly predicts symbolic and visual state transitions to provide synchronized bilevel guidance for VLA execution on extended manipulation tasks.
novelty: Combines a learned logical world model that proposes and scores state-action trajectories with a visually grounded latent subgoal model invoked once per subtask.
limitations: Requires logically synchronized annotations, predicate classifiers, and manual screening; symbolic abstractions and subtask boundaries remain task-engineered, and the system is evaluated on a limited real-world setup.
future_work: Reduce dependence on manually aligned symbolic annotations, improve open-world state estimation, and broaden real-world deployment and task diversity.
evidence_world_model: The logical module predicts structured state transitions and the visual module predicts the perceptual latent corresponding to the resulting symbolic state.
evidence_planning: The logical world model proposes candidate logical actions and predicted transitions and scores partial trajectories for consistency and goal alignment.
evidence_horizon: The paper explicitly evaluates five- to twenty-step long-horizon tasks and an eight-step physical task.
review_flags: Closest overall match to the proposed research direction. It is hierarchical but sequence/subtask based rather than a recursive graph-of-graphs model.

title: Semantic-Geometric-Physical-Driven Robot Manipulation Skill Transfer via Skill Library and Tactile Representation
authors: Mingchao Qi; Yuanjin Li; Xing Liu; Zhengxiong Liu; Panfeng Huang
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2411.11714 on 18 Nov 2024
open_source_notes: Project code and prompts are released in the skill_transfer GitHub repository.
task_domain: Long-Horizon Manipulation; Skill Transfer; Contact-Rich Manipulation
primary_problem: Knowledge-Graph Planning; Skill Transfer; Tactile Adaptation
world_model_detail: The framework represents task, scene, and state knowledge explicitly in Neo4j graphs but does not learn a predictive environment-transition model.
planning_method: Hierarchical Language Planner; A*; Knowledge-Graph Retrieval
planning_representation: Task graph, scene graph, state graph, and executable action-primitive sequences
horizon_basis: Long-horizon tasks are decomposed into subtasks, each containing sequential low-level action primitives.
replanning_online: Qualified — the transferred task and trajectories are adapted to the new scene, but continuous global replanning is not the paper's focus
learning_type: In-Context LLM Reasoning; Knowledge-Graph Skill Transfer; Tactile Geometry Processing
policy_type: Skill Library with Motion Planner and Tactile Correction
observation_modality: Scene-graph geometry; object pose; GelSight tactile images
goal_representation: Transferred subtask sequence and scene-conditioned geometric targets
action_space: Action primitives and Cartesian end-effector trajectories
benchmark: Novel drawer-to-cabinet transfer involving door opening, cup retrieval, transport, and stacking
robot_platform: KUKA LBR iiwa14 with GelSight Mini
main_contribution: Organizes reusable manipulation knowledge as linked task, scene, and state graphs and transfers it through semantic, geometric, and tactile levels.
novelty: Explicitly connects high-level subtasks to contained action primitives and uses a state graph to map task requirements to scene relations.
limitations: Cannot model deformable objects, and the 2D-pretrained LLM can generate inaccurate 3D motion paths; evaluation is limited to a narrow transfer scenario.
future_work: Introduce 3D inputs such as point clouds into model adaptation and extend the framework to deformable and more diverse contact-rich objects.
evidence_world_model: The graphs store task and scene knowledge but do not predict learned action-conditioned future environment states.
evidence_planning: An LLM transfers the subtask sequence, A* adapts motion trajectories, and tactile estimates correct final contact pose.
evidence_horizon: The demonstrated transfer chains opening, grasping, pulling, releasing, retrieving, moving, and precise stacking.
review_flags: Very close structural precedent for a graph-of-graphs hierarchy, but it is a knowledge-graph skill system rather than a learned hierarchical world model.

title: Semantic–Geometric Task Representations for Bimanual Manipulation from Human Demonstrations to Robot Action Planning
authors: Franziska Herbert; Vignesh Prasad; Han Liu; Dorothea Koert; Georgia Chalvatzaki
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2601.11460 on 16 Jan 2026; supplied revision v2 dated 30 May 2026
venue: arXiv / submitted to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
open_source_notes: A public project website accompanies the paper.
task_domain: Bimanual Manipulation; Learning from Human Demonstrations; Task Graphs
primary_problem: Graph World Modeling; Task Progression Forecasting; Online Action Planning
world_model_detail: An MPNN encodes temporal object-centric scene graphs containing identities, semantic relations, motion histories, and task context; transformer decoders forecast future actions, involved objects, and continuous object motions.
predictive_space: Hybrid symbolic and object-centric geometric state
prediction_target: Future bimanual action sequence, target objects, and 3D object motions
action_conditioned: Yes — the decoder conditions on action context
planning_representation: Semantic–geometric temporal task graph and predicted ProMP consistency
horizon_basis: The learned decoder forecasts multi-step task progression across extended bimanual action sequences rather than a single action.
hierarchical_planning: Yes — learned action-object selection guides parameterized low-level Probabilistic Movement Primitives
replanning_online: Yes — the graph and candidate action-object predictions are recomputed online between primitives
learning_type: Human-Demonstration Pretraining; Graph Representation Learning; Decoder-Only Robot Finetuning
policy_type: Online Planner with ProMP Skill Library
pretraining: Yes — representation learning from bimanual human demonstrations before small-data robot finetuning
observation_modality: Third-person RGB-D; tracked 3D object and hand positions; semantic relations; task ID
goal_representation: Task identity and predicted semantic–geometric progression
action_space: Bimanual categorical action primitives with parameterized ProMP trajectories
benchmark: Eleven bimanual tasks from KIT Bimacs and an additional Bimacs-style dataset; real cooking and clear-up tasks
robot_platform: Physical bimanual tabletop robot with parameterized ProMP skill library
main_contribution: Learns a reusable graph embedding that jointly captures task semantics and geometric evolution and transfers it from humans to online bimanual robot planning.
novelty: Separates task-agnostic graph encoding from action-conditioned forecasting, enabling decoder-only embodiment adaptation and joint prediction of symbolic and continuous futures.
limitations: Uses only 3D positions rather than 6D poses, requires tracked objects and heuristic relations, and needs small robot-specific finetuning; motion prediction currently serves only action scoring.
future_work: Add 6D object poses, use VLM perception to construct graph features, and connect the representation to VLA low-level policies.
evidence_world_model: The graph predictor forecasts future object motions and task actions from the current temporal relational state.
evidence_planning: The online planner scores candidate skills using predicted action likelihood and consistency between predicted object motion and learned ProMPs.
evidence_horizon: The work explicitly targets long-horizon task progression and evaluates multi-step bimanual tasks with varying action ordering.
review_flags: Closest learned graph-embedding precedent. Unlike the proposed idea, it has one semantic-geometric level rather than recursively nested task, primitive, and trajectory graphs.

title: From Reaction to Anticipation: Proactive Failure Recovery through Agentic Task Graph for Robotic Manipulation
authors: Sheng Xu; Ruixing Jin; Huayi Zhou; Bo Yue; Guanren Qiao; Yueci Deng; Yunxin Tai; Kui Jia; Guiliang Liu
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2605.11951 on 12 May 2026; accepted to RSS 2026
open_source_notes: Code, prompts, and examples are publicly released in the AgentChord repository.
task_domain: Long-Horizon Manipulation; Failure Recovery; Bimanual Manipulation
primary_problem: Task-Graph Planning; Proactive Failure Recovery; Execution Monitoring
world_model_detail: The system builds a semantic task graph and recovery branches but does not learn a predictive environment-transition model.
planning_method: Agentic Task Graph; Hierarchical Constrained Solvers; Recovery Planning
planning_representation: Directed nominal graph augmented with failure contexts, recovery nodes, merge points, and monitored transitions
horizon_basis: The graph represents complete single-arm and bimanual tasks with multiple subgoals and forward-moving recovery branches.
replanning_online: Yes — monitors trigger precompiled recovery branches and rejoin the nominal graph without invoking a new MLLM plan
learning_type: MLLM Agentic Reasoning; Compiled Geometric Monitoring; Constraint-Based Execution
pretraining: Yes — pretrained multimodal language models provide graph construction and failure anticipation
observation_modality: Natural language; multi-view RGB-D; geometric scene features
goal_representation: Semantic subgoal graph with nominal and recovery constraints
action_space: Atomic robot actions compiled into single- and dual-arm trajectories
benchmark: Simulation tasks and six real tasks: single/dual-arm pouring, table rearrangement, block handover, towel folding, and coffee-tray setup
robot_platform: AgileX CobotMagic dual-arm robot with two binocular RGB-D cameras
main_contribution: Augments a nominal manipulation graph before execution with anticipated, context-specific, forward-moving recovery branches and low-latency monitors.
novelty: Moves failure reasoning offline into graph construction so execution can immediately branch to a corrective route rather than repeatedly invoking an MLLM or backtracking.
limitations: Rare or compound failures may be missed, reasoning errors can attach recovery to the wrong node, IK may make proposed recoveries infeasible, and geometric monitoring is weak for irregular, deformable, or transparent objects.
future_work: Add fallback reasoning for unforeseen failures and stronger semantic-geometric, point-cloud, and relational-keypoint feature extractors.
evidence_world_model: AgentChord predicts likely failure modes conceptually but does not learn state-transition dynamics or future environment observations.
evidence_planning: Composer, arranger, and conductor agents construct, augment, compile, monitor, and execute the directed task and recovery graph.
evidence_horizon: Six long-horizon real tasks are evaluated under randomized external disturbances, including asynchronous and synchronous bimanual coordination.
review_flags: Highly relevant to adding contingency and recovery branches to the proposed task graph, but not itself a world model.

title: RoboHorizon: An LLM-Assisted Multi-View World Model for Long-Horizon Robotic Manipulation
authors: Zixuan Chen; Jing Huo; Yangtao Chen; Yang Gao
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2501.06605 in Jan 2025; supplied web version v3
open_source_notes: No public implementation repository was identified on the paper page.
primary_problem: World Modeling; LLM Reward Generation; Multi-View Representation Learning; Policy Learning
world_model_detail: An RSSM consumes frozen key-horizon multi-view MAE features, previous latent state, and action to predict future latent states, reconstructed observations, and LLM-generated staged rewards.
prediction_target: Future latent states, reconstructed key-horizon visual features, and dense rewards
video_prediction: No — reconstructs key-horizon visual representations rather than generating full videos
planning_representation: RSSM imagined trajectories with stage-aware rewards and values
horizon_basis: The actor's imagination horizon is matched to variable-length key horizons for sequential long-horizon subtasks and furniture assembly.
hierarchical_planning: Yes — LLM-generated staged task rewards organize lower-level imagined policy optimization
replanning_online: Yes — the learned actor acts from current observations while the RSSM provides imagined future values during training
learning_type: Multi-View Masked Autoencoding; RSSM Learning; Dreamer-Style Actor-Critic; Behavior Cloning
pretraining: Yes — multi-view representation learning and LLM-generated reward coding precede policy optimization
observation_modality: Multi-view RGB during training; front-view RGB for policy execution; natural-language task description
goal_representation: LLM-generated multi-stage dense and intermediate rewards
benchmark: Four short and six long RLBench tasks plus FurnitureBench cabinet, lamp, and round-table assembly
robot_platform: Simulated Franka Panda environments in RLBench and FurnitureBench
main_contribution: Combines LLM-generated stage-aware rewards, key-horizon multi-view perception, and an RSSM actor-critic to improve long-horizon manipulation learning.
novelty: Extends Sense-Plan-Act into Recognize-Sense-Plan-Act, using language to define stage structure and keyframe-aware visual learning to improve long-horizon model-based RL.
limitations: The reward-generation loop lacks human feedback, all evaluation is simulated, and the system uses only RGB without proprioception or depth.
future_work: Close the LLM reward-generation loop with human feedback and deploy the framework on real long-horizon manipulation tasks.
evidence_world_model: The RSSM predicts future latent states from current representations, previous state, and action and learns observation reconstruction and reward prediction.
evidence_planning: A DreamerV2-style actor and critic optimize predicted returns by backpropagating through imagined RSSM trajectories.
evidence_horizon: The paper evaluates six long-horizon RLBench tasks and three furniture-assembly tasks and adapts imagination duration to task-stage key horizons.
review_flags: Relevant LLM-plus-world-model long-horizon baseline, but its hierarchy enters through staged rewards and key horizons rather than explicit nested task graphs.
